_meta.description: Evidence-first, non-accusatory documentation of UK public-sector probabilistic record linkage (mainly MoJ Splink). Each link is a sourced fact carrier; status_flag is a human pre-flag, re-verified by scripts/source_checker.py.

superseded.title: Data First: Harnessing the potential of linked administrative data for the justice system
superseded.reason: Seed URL returned HTTP 404 on three independent fetches (Phase 0 checker, WebFetch x2) on 2026-06-10. Still indexed by search engines (stale index). Retired from the active checked set to keep DEAD a meaningful regression signal. Superseded by S18 (gov.uk Data First landing) and S32 (ADR UK ethics page). C02 remains supported by S03.

unverified_dropped.title: Understanding the National Data Opt-Out
unverified_dropped.reason: Returns HTTP 403 to every automated client available here (checker + WebFetch). Appears live in search index but resolution could not be independently confirmed, so not recorded per Hard Rule 1. National Data Opt-Out point is instead sourced to the accessible HRA page S28. Re-add after manual browser verification.

unverified_dropped.title: Access data on the Personal Demographics Service
unverified_dropped.reason: HTTP 403 to all automated clients here; resolution not independently confirmed, so not recorded. PDS linkage is covered by S14 and the lawful-basis context by S38. Re-add after manual browser verification.

unverified_dropped.title: What does the UK GDPR say about automated decision-making and profiling? (ICO subpage)
unverified_dropped.reason: Returned HTTP 404 (checker) / 403 (WebFetch); exact path unstable. DUAA 2025 Art.22 -> 22A-22D change is instead sourced to the accessible ICO DUAA summary S35 and legislation.gov.uk S42.

links.title: Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (2025 c. 18)
links.note: Primary text of the Act amending UK GDPR/DPA 2018 (Art.22 -> 22A-22D; Art.89 -> 84B). Verified resolves 2026-06-10; specific section text to be cited in Phase 3.

links.title: UK GDPR Article 22A — Automated processing and significant decisions
links.note: Verified by fetch 2026-06-10: title 'Automated processing and significant decisions'; Chapter 3 Section 4A (Arts 22A-22D) inserted by DUAA 2025, in force 5.2.2026 (19.6.2025 for specified purposes). 22A is definitions only (significant decision; 'solely automated' = no meaningful human involvement).

links.title: UK GDPR Article 22B — Restrictions on automated significant decision-making
links.note: Operative restriction (inserted by DUAA 2025). Read 2026-06-10: solely-automated significant decisions based wholly/partly on special-category data are PROHIBITED save narrow exceptions (e.g. explicit consent); for ordinary personal data the former blanket bar is relaxed, restricted mainly where relying on the new Art 6(1)(ea) basis, subject to Art 22C safeguards.

links.title: UK GDPR Article 22C — Safeguards for permitted automated significant decisions
links.note: Inserted by DUAA 2025. Read 2026-06-10: controller must (a) inform the data subject, (b) enable representations, (c) enable human intervention, (d) enable contesting the decision. May be expanded by Art 22D(3) regs.

links.title: UK GDPR Article 22D — Supplementary / Secretary of State regulation-making power
links.note: Inserted by DUAA 2025. Read 2026-06-10: SoS may by regs provide whether there is/ is not 'meaningful human involvement', whether a decision has 'similarly significant effect', and add/modify how 22C(2) safeguards operate (incl. defining measures that do NOT satisfy them). Affirmative resolution procedure; cannot directly amend 22C (para 4).

links.title: UK GDPR Article 6 — incl. new point (1)(ea) 'recognised legitimate interest'
links.note: Read 2026-06-10: Art 6(1)(ea) 'processing is necessary for the purposes of a recognised legitimate interest', inserted by DUAA 2025 (in force 5.2.2026). Per Art 6(5) only if it meets a condition in Annex 1; SoS may amend Annex 1 by regs.

links.title: UK GDPR Article 22 (substituted) — substitution note
links.note: Read 2026-06-10: 'Ch. 3 Section 4A substituted for Art. 22' by DUAA 2025 (eff 19.6.2025 specified purposes / 5.2.2026 broadly). Original Art 22 heading 'Automated individual decision-making, including profiling'. Consequential changes to Arts 4, 13, 14, 15 noted as outstanding.

links.title: UK GDPR Annex 1 — Recognised legitimate interests (Art. 6(1)(ea))
links.note: Inserted by DUAA 2025 (eff 5.2.2026). Read 2026-06-10: categories incl. disclosure for 6(1)(e) processing; national security/public security/defence; emergencies (Civil Contingencies Act 2004); crime (detect/investigate/prevent; apprehend/prosecute offenders); safeguarding vulnerable individuals. SoS may amend Annex 1 by regs.

links.title: UK GDPR Article 14 — information where data not obtained from the data subject (as amended)
links.note: Read 2026-06-10: DUAA 2025 amended Art 14(5) exemptions (impossible/disproportionate effort; likely to render impossible/seriously impair objectives) and added a 'make information available publicly' protective measure. Apparent restructuring of the research exemption — VERIFY VERBATIM before load-bearing use.

links.title: MoJ: Splink Master Record — Algorithmic Transparency Record
links.note: Fetched & read 2026-06-10. Distinct from the Data First ATR (S19). Describes OPERATIONAL, individual-affecting Splink uses: 'Core Person Record' (real-time unique IDs across CJS), 'Probation in Court' (retrieve probation records when an individual appears in court), and a POLICE data-sharing pilot linking to PNC numbers for arrest-info requests; plus research (JustLink). Sources: DELIUS, NOMIS, Common Platform, LIBRA, FamilyMan, CaseMan. Names Spark as backend (per the related gov.uk publication), NOT DuckDB. Read 2026-06-10 (deeper): DPIAs/DPIA screenings done per use case but NOT published/linked; states Splink 'does not directly make decisions about individuals' — human makes final decision on candidate matches (human-in-the-loop); controller + lawful basis NOT stated; PNC pilot sends numbers to police daily, human-check/onward-use unspecified. Splink v4.0.8.

links.title: HMPPS fair processing notice (prisons)
links.note: Live (HTTP 200, checker). Attempted full read 2026-06-10: binary/image PDF, could NOT text-extract. Part 3 'competent authority' basis is from the search index only, not personally read — claim C32 stays LIKELY; manual read or FOI needed to confirm verbatim.

links.title: National Probation Service Privacy Notice
links.note: Live (HTTP 200, checker). Attempted full read 2026-06-10: binary/image PDF, could NOT text-extract. Probation-side processing context only; not personally read.

links.title: DWP 'fairness analysis' reveals bias in AI fraud detection system
links.note: Read 2026-06-11. DWP's own Feb-2024 fairness analysis found STATISTICALLY SIGNIFICANT disparity in its ML tool flagging Universal Credit claimants for fraud, across age/disability/marital status/nationality (race/sex/orientation/religion NOT analysed). DWP: human caseworkers review (not solely-automated). 11-page assessment released under FOI to the Public Law Project.

links.title: UK: Ministry of Justice secretly developing 'murder prediction' system
links.note: Read 2026-06-11. MoJ 'Homicide Prediction Project' to predict who will commit murder, using PNC + Greater Manchester Police data (100k-500k people), MoJ datasets, and 'health markers' (mental health, addiction, self-harm, suicide, disability) said to have 'significant predictive power'. Covers suspects, VICTIMS, WITNESSES, missing/safeguarding people. Bodies: MoJ, GMP, Met, West Midlands (discussions), Home Office. Statewatch obtained DPIA + data-sharing agreement via FOI. Bias: similar tools rank Black offenders less accurately than white.

links.title: Algorithmic Transparency Records (ATRS registry)
links.note: Searchable registry of UK public-sector algorithmic tools. ~125 records published by Apr 2026; ATRS now mandatory for govt departments. Policing not mandatory but 2 police records exist (Hampshire & Thames Valley: DARAT; West Midlands: sexual-convictions analysis). The catalogue to mine for further linkage/profiling tools.
